sections.title: Data Structures Overview
sections.content:
## Arrays
- Fixed size with contiguous memory allocation
- Constant time access to elements using index
- Insertion and deletion require shifting elements, thus O(n) time
- Suitable for storing multiple elements of the same data type

## Linked Lists
- Non-contiguous memory allocation
- Access time is O(n) as traversal needed from head
- Insertion and deletion are O(1) if position is already known
- Types: Singly Linked List, Doubly Linked List, Circular Linked List
- Advantage: Dynamic size and flexible memory usage
- Disadvantage: Not cache friendly and requires extra memory for pointers

## Stacks (LIFO - Last In First Out)
- Last element inserted is first to be removed
- Operations: Push (add), Pop (remove), Peek (view top) all in O(1) time
- Applications: Expression evaluation, function call stack, undo/redo functionality
- Can be implemented using arrays or linked lists

## Queues (FIFO - First In First Out)
- First element inserted is first to be removed
- Operations: Enqueue (add) and Dequeue (remove) both O(1) time
- Types: Simple Queue, Circular Queue, Priority Queue, Deque
- Applications: Process scheduling, breadth-first search, message queuing

## Trees
- Hierarchical data structure with one root node
- Key terms: Root (top node), Leaf (no children), Height (max edges from root to leaf), Depth (edges from root to node)
- Binary Tree: Each node has maximum 2 children
- Full Binary Tree: Every node has either 0 or 2 children
- Complete Binary Tree: All levels filled except possibly the last level
- Perfect Binary Tree: All internal nodes have exactly 2 children and all leaves at same level

## Binary Search Trees (BST)
- Left subtree contains values less than parent
- Right subtree contains values greater than parent
- Search, Insert, Delete operations take O(log n) average time
- Worst case is O(n) when tree becomes skewed
- Used for maintaining sorted data and fast lookups

## Hash Tables
- Key-value pairs using hash function to map keys to indices
- Average access time is O(1)
- Collisions occur when hash function maps multiple keys to same index
- Collision handling: Chaining (linked list at each index) and Open Addressing (find another slot)
- Load factor is ratio of elements to table size
- Rehashing occurs when load factor exceeds threshold typically 0.75

## Graphs
- Collection of nodes connected by edges
- Directed Graph: Edges have direction with arrows
- Undirected Graph: Edges have no direction
- Weighted Graph: Edges have associated weights or costs
- Representation: Adjacency Matrix uses O(V squared) space, Adjacency List uses O(V plus E) space

## Heaps
- Complete binary tree with heap property
- Max Heap: Parent value is greater than or equal to children
- Min Heap: Parent value is less than or equal to children
- Insert and Delete operations take O(log n) time
- Extract maximum or minimum takes O(1) time
- Used in Priority Queues and Heap Sort algorithm

sections.title: Algorithms and Complexity
sections.content:
## Time Complexity Analysis
- Measure of how execution time grows with input size
- Big O Notation represents worst-case scenario
- Common complexities in increasing order: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n squared), O(n cubed), O(2 to power n), O(n factorial)
- O(1) means constant time regardless of input size
- O(n) means linear time proportional to input
- O(log n) means logarithmic time found in binary search

## Space Complexity Analysis
- Measure of extra memory required by algorithm
- Includes auxiliary space beyond input data
- Important consideration in memory-constrained environments
- Often a trade-off exists between time and space complexity

## Asymptotic Notations
- Big O notation represents upper bound or worst case
- Omega notation represents lower bound or best case
- Theta notation represents tight bound or average case
- Used to classify algorithms by their efficiency

## Divide and Conquer Strategy
- Break problem into smaller subproblems
- Solve subproblems independently
- Combine solutions to get final answer
- Examples: Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search
- Recurrence relation: T(n) equals a times T(n/b) plus f(n)
- Master Theorem provides method to solve recurrence relations

## Greedy Algorithms
- Make locally optimal choice at each step
- Aim to find global optimum solution
- Efficient but no guarantee of global optimum
- Examples: Activity Selection, Huffman Coding, Dijkstra shortest path, Prim's Minimum Spanning Tree
- Used when greedy choice leads to optimal solution

## Dynamic Programming
- Solves problems with optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems
- Memoization: Top-down approach storing computed results
- Tabulation: Bottom-up approach building solution from base cases
- Examples: Fibonacci sequence, Longest Common Subsequence, 0/1 Knapsack, Matrix Chain Multiplication
- Trades space for time efficiency

## Backtracking Approach
- Explore all possible solutions by trying each option
- Abandon current path when constraint is violated
- Return to previous step and try alternative path
- Examples: N-Queens problem, Sudoku Solver, Permutations
- Time complexity is typically exponential

## NP Completeness
- P problems are solvable in polynomial time
- NP problems are verifiable in polynomial time
- NP-Complete problems are in NP and as hard as any NP problem
- Examples: Boolean Satisfiability, Traveling Salesman Problem, Subset Sum
- No known polynomial time solution for NP-Complete problems
- Whether P equals NP remains unsolved major problem

sections.title: Sorting and Searching
sections.content:
## Bubble Sort
- Repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps if in wrong order
- Largest element bubbles to end in each pass
- Time Complexity: O(n squared) worst case, O(n squared) average case, O(n) best case
- Space Complexity: O(1) as no extra space needed
- Stable sort: maintains relative order of equal elements
- Suitable for small datasets only

## Selection Sort
- Find minimum element and place at beginning
- Repeat for remaining unsorted portion
- Time Complexity: O(n squared) in all cases
- Space Complexity: O(1)
- Unstable sort: may change relative order of equal elements
- Number of comparisons is always n times (n minus 1) divided by 2

## Insertion Sort
- Build sorted array one element at a time
- Insert each new element into correct position
- Time Complexity: O(n squared) worst and average, O(n) best case
- Space Complexity: O(1)
- Stable sort
- Efficient for small datasets and nearly sorted arrays

## Merge Sort
- Divide array into halves recursively
- Merge the sorted halves back together
- Time Complexity: O(n log n) in all cases
- Space Complexity: O(n) for temporary arrays
- Stable sort
- Preferred for linked lists due to no random access needed
- Guarantees O(n log n) performance

## Quick Sort
- Select pivot element and partition array
- Recursively sort left and right partitions
- Time Complexity: O(n log n) average, O(n squared) worst case
- Space Complexity: O(log n) average for recursion stack
- Unstable sort
- Fastest average case in practice
- Pivot selection crucial: random, median-of-three, or first element

## Heap Sort
- Build max heap from array
- Extract maximum element and place at end
- Repeat for remaining elements
- Time Complexity: O(n log n) in all cases
- Space Complexity: O(1) as sorting done in place
- Unstable sort
- Guarantees O(n log n) performance

## Counting Sort
- Count occurrences of each value
- Place elements in order based on counts
- Time Complexity: O(n plus k) where k is range of values
- Space Complexity: O(k)
- Stable sort
- Suitable for integers with limited range
- Not comparison-based sorting

## Radix Sort
- Sort by individual digits or characters
- Process from least significant to most significant
- Time Complexity: O(n times k) where k is number of digits
- Space Complexity: O(n plus k)
- Stable sort
- Fast for large numbers with many digits
- Uses counting sort as subroutine

## Linear Search
- Scan each element sequentially until found
- Time Complexity: O(n)
- Space Complexity: O(1)
- Works on unsorted or small arrays
- No preprocessing required

## Binary Search
- Divide sorted array in half repeatedly
- Compare target with middle element
- Search in appropriate half based on comparison
- Time Complexity: O(log n)
- Space Complexity: O(1) for iterative, O(log n) for recursive
- Requires array to be sorted beforehand
- Much faster than linear search for large datasets

## Ternary Search
- Divide sorted array into three parts
- Eliminate two thirds based on comparisons
- Time Complexity: O(log base 3 of n)
- Rarely used as binary search is simpler and similar

sections.title: Operating System Concepts
sections.content:
## Processes versus Threads
- Process: Independent program instance with own memory space
- Each process typically gets 4GB of memory space
- Thread: Lightweight unit within process, shares memory
- Multiple threads in single process enable parallelism
- Thread creation and context switching faster than process
- Threads communicate through shared memory more easily
- Processes are isolated and protected from each other

## CPU Scheduling
- Determines which process gets CPU at any given time
- Preemptive: OS can interrupt running process
- Non-preemptive: Process runs to completion
- First Come First Served: Simple, follows arrival order, causes convoy effect
- Shortest Job First: Minimizes average wait time, risk of starvation for long jobs
- Round Robin: Fair allocation with time quantum, good for interactive systems
- Priority Scheduling: Higher priority jobs run first, risk of starvation
- Multilevel Queue: Different priority classes for different job types

## Deadlock
- Situation where processes wait for each other indefinitely
- Four conditions must all be present: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait
- Prevention: Break one of four conditions
- Detection: Use resource allocation graph
- Recovery: Abort process or preempt resources
- Example: Two processes each holding resource and waiting for other's resource

## Synchronization and Mutual Exclusion
- Critical Section: Code that accesses shared resource
- Mutex: Binary semaphore with value 0 or 1
- Semaphore: Counter-based synchronization primitive
- Race Condition: Unpredictable outcome due to concurrent access
- Solutions: Locks, semaphores, monitors, atomic operations
- Busy waiting: Process continuously checks condition, wastes CPU

## Memory Management
- Virtual Memory: Program uses logical addresses mapped to physical memory
- Paging: Divide memory into fixed-size blocks called pages
- Segmentation: Divide memory into variable-size blocks
- Page Replacement Algorithms: LRU (Least Recently Used), FIFO (First In First Out), Optimal
- Thrashing: Excessive paging reduces performance significantly
- Page Fault: Occurs when referenced page not in physical memory

## File System
- Directories: Organize files hierarchically in tree structure
- Inode: File metadata including permissions, size, timestamps, pointers to data blocks
- File System Types: FAT (legacy), NTFS (Windows), ext4 (Linux)
- Fragmentation: Scattered data blocks reduce access speed
- Defragmentation: Reorganize data to restore continuity
- Access Control: Read, Write, Execute permissions for Owner, Group, Others

## Shell and Command Line
- Command Interpreter: Translates user input to operating system actions
- Pipes: Connect output of one command to input of another
- Redirection: Send output to file or read input from file
- Background Execution: Run process in background allowing further commands
- Wildcards: Match patterns in filenames
- Standard Streams: stdin (input), stdout (output), stderr (errors)

sections.title: Computer Networks Basics
sections.content:
## OSI Model Seven Layers
- Layer 1 Physical: Cables, hubs, electrical signals, physical medium
- Layer 2 Data Link: MAC addresses, switches, Ethernet, frame formatting
- Layer 3 Network: IP addresses, routers, routing protocols (RIP, OSPF, BGP)
- Layer 4 Transport: TCP, UDP, ports, reliable end-to-end delivery
- Layer 5 Session: Session management, dialog control, synchronization
- Layer 6 Presentation: Encryption, compression, data format conversion
- Layer 7 Application: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH protocols

## TCP/IP Model
- Four layers: Link, Internet, Transport, Application
- More practical implementation compared to OSI model
- Combines some OSI layers into fewer layers
- Currently most widely used reference model

## IP Addressing
- IPv4: 32-bit address, represented as four decimal numbers (0 to 255)
- Example: 192.168.1.1
- IPv6: 128-bit address, represented as eight groups of hex digits
- Classful Addressing: Class A (0 to 126), Class B (128 to 191), Class C (192 to 223)
- Classless Addressing (CIDR): Uses forward slash notation like /24
- Subnet Mask: Determines which portion is network and which is host
- Default Gateway: Router that connects local network to other networks

## TCP versus UDP
- TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):
  - Reliable delivery with acknowledgments
  - Ordered delivery of packets
  - Connection-oriented with three-way handshake
  - Slower due to error checking
  - Used for HTTP, FTP, Email
- UDP (User Datagram Protocol):
  - Unreliable delivery, no acknowledgments
  - No guaranteed order
  - Connectionless with no handshake
  - Faster due to minimal overhead
  - Used for DNS, video streaming, online gaming

## Domain Name System (DNS)
- Resolves domain names to IP addresses
- Hierarchical system: Root servers, TLD servers, Authoritative nameservers
- DNS Records: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail server)
- Recursive Query: Full resolution by DNS resolver
- Iterative Query: Referral to next nameserver
- Caching: Reduces queries to authoritative servers

## HTTP versus HTTPS
- HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol):
  - Port 80
  - Unencrypted communication
  - Vulnerable to eavesdropping
- HTTPS (HTTP Secure):
  - Port 443
  - Encrypted using TLS or SSL protocols
  - Verifies server authenticity
  - Ensures confidentiality and integrity
- Status Codes:
  - 2xx (Success): Request succeeded
  - 3xx (Redirect): Further action needed
  - 4xx (Client Error): Invalid request
  - 5xx (Server Error): Server failed to process valid request

## Routing
- Static Routing: Administrator manually configures routes
- Dynamic Routing: Protocols automatically determine best paths
- Distance Vector Routing: RIP (Routing Information Protocol)
- Link State Routing: OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)
- Border Gateway Protocol: BGP for Internet routing between ASNs
- Routing Table: Maps destinations to next hops

## Bandwidth and Latency
- Bandwidth: Data transfer capacity measured in bits per second
- Latency: Time for data to travel from source to destination
- Throughput: Actual data transferred, affected by congestion
- Jitter: Variation in latency over time
- Network performance depends on both bandwidth and latency

sections.title: DBMS Fundamentals
sections.content:
## Database Models
- Relational: Data organized in tables with rows and columns, uses SQL
- Most common and widely used model
- NoSQL: Flexible schema, includes document, key-value, and graph databases
- Hierarchical: Tree structure (outdated)
- Network: Graph structure for complex relationships

## ACID Properties
- Atomicity: Transaction treated as all or nothing, no partial updates
- Consistency: Database moves from one valid state to another valid state
- Isolation: Concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other
- Durability: Once committed, data persists even after failures
- These properties ensure data reliability and integrity

## Normalization
- Remove data redundancy through systematic process
- First Normal Form: Atomic values, no repeating groups
- Second Normal Form: First NF plus no partial dependencies
- Third Normal Form: Second NF plus no transitive dependencies
- Boyce-Codd Normal Form: Stricter than 3NF
- Denormalization: Intentional redundancy for performance improvement

## Database Keys
- Primary Key: Unique identifier for each record, cannot be null
- Foreign Key: Links record to another table's primary key
- Candidate Key: Potential primary key choice
- Super Key: Any superset of candidate key
- Surrogate Key: Artificial primary key with no business meaning
- Composite Key: Primary key consisting of multiple columns

## Join Operations
- INNER JOIN: Returns only matching records from both tables
- LEFT JOIN: All records from left table plus matching from right
- RIGHT JOIN: All records from right table plus matching from left
- FULL OUTER JOIN: All records from both tables
- CROSS JOIN: Cartesian product of both tables
- Self Join: Table joined with itself

## Indexes
- Data structure for faster data retrieval
- B-Tree Index: Balanced tree structure, suitable for range queries
- Hash Index: Hash function based, good for equality queries
- Primary Index: Created on primary key automatically
- Secondary Index: Created on non-key columns
- Trade-off: Faster queries but slower insert, update, delete operations

## Transactions
- COMMIT: Saves all changes permanently
- ROLLBACK: Undoes all changes in transaction
- SAVEPOINT: Allows partial rollback to specific point
- Locking: Shared lock for reading, Exclusive lock for writing
- Deadlock: Possible with multiple locks on resources
- Transaction Isolation Levels: Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable

## Backup and Recovery
- Full Backup: Complete copy of all data
- Incremental Backup: Only changes since last backup
- Differential Backup: Changes since last full backup
- RAID: Redundant Array of Independent Disks for fault tolerance
- Recovery Time Objective (RTO): Target time for recovery
- Recovery Point Objective (RPO): Maximum acceptable data loss

## SQL Basics
- DDL (Data Definition Language): CREATE, ALTER, DROP tables and structures
- DML (Data Manipulation Language): SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE data
- DCL (Data Control Language): GRANT, REVOKE permissions
- TCL (Transaction Control Language): COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT
- WHERE Clause: Filter records based on conditions
- ORDER BY: Sort results in ascending or descending order
- GROUP BY: Group records by column values
- HAVING: Filter groups based on conditions

sections.title: Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)
sections.content:
## Waterfall Model
- Sequential phases: Requirements, Design, Development, Testing, Deployment
- Each phase must complete before next begins
- Rigid approach with heavy documentation
- Suitable for projects with fixed and well-understood requirements
- Risk: Testing occurs late, making bug fixes expensive
- Limited customer interaction after requirements phase

## Iterative Model
- Multiple cycles of development
- Customer feedback incorporated in each iteration
- Allows for design and requirement changes
- Flexible approach adapting to changing needs
- Risk: Scope creep if changes not managed properly

## Agile Methodology
- Short development cycles called sprints (1 to 4 weeks)
- Continuous delivery and customer feedback
- Adaptive planning and continuous improvement
- Scrum Framework: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Development Team
- Kanban: Continuous flow of work through stages
- Benefits: Fast delivery, customer collaboration, quick adaptation
- Challenge: Requires discipline and experienced team

## DevOps Approach
- Integration of Development and Operations teams
- Continuous Integration: Automated testing and frequent code merges
- Continuous Deployment: Automated release to production
- Tools: Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions
- Culture of collaboration, automation, and shared responsibility
- Goal: Faster, more reliable software delivery

## SDLC Phases
- Planning: Define project scope, feasibility study, resource allocation, timeline
- Requirements: Gather functional and non-functional requirements
- Design: System architecture, database schema, UI mockups, technical specifications
- Development: Actual code implementation following design documents
- Testing: Unit, Integration, System, and User Acceptance Testing
- Deployment: Release to production, user training, documentation
- Maintenance: Bug fixes, updates, performance monitoring, support

## Testing Types
- Unit Testing: Individual component testing by developer
- Integration Testing: Testing interaction between components
- System Testing: Testing complete system functionality
- User Acceptance Testing: End user validates against requirements
- Functional Testing: Verify features work as specified
- Performance Testing: Measure speed and responsiveness
- Security Testing: Identify vulnerabilities
- Regression Testing: Ensure new changes do not break existing features

## Version Control Systems
- Git: Distributed version control, supports branching and merging
- SVN: Centralized version control (less popular now)
- Commits: Track changes with messages
- Branches: Parallel development lines for features
- Merge: Combine changes from different branches
- Merge Conflicts: Occur when same code modified in different branches
- GitFlow: Structured branching model with develop and release branches
- GitHub Flow: Simple model with feature branches and pull requests

## Code Quality
- Code Reviews: Peer examination before merging
- Static Analysis: Automated code quality checks
- Testing Coverage: Aim for greater than 80 percent coverage
- Refactoring: Improve code without changing behavior
- Technical Debt: Shortcuts creating future problems
- Linting: Automated style and error checking

sections.title: Cloud and DevOps Basics
sections.content:
## Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)
- Virtualized computing resources over the internet
- Rent servers, storage, and networking infrastructure
- Examples: AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform
- Customer manages: Operating system, middleware, applications, data
- Provider manages: Hardware, virtualization, physical infrastructure
- Cost-effective: Pay only for used resources
- Scalable: Easily add or remove resources

## Platform as a Service (PaaS)
- Development platform with integrated tools and services
- Build and deploy applications without managing infrastructure
- Examples: Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine
- Customer manages: Applications and data only
- Provider manages: Everything else including platform and infrastructure
- Faster development and deployment
- Reduces complexity of infrastructure management

## Software as a Service (SaaS)
- Fully managed applications accessed via web browser
- No installation or maintenance required
- Examples: Salesforce, Microsoft 365, Google Workspace
- Customer only uses the application
- Provider manages: Everything including servers, maintenance, updates
- Lower upfront costs
- Accessible from anywhere with internet

## Cloud Deployment Models
- Public Cloud: Shared resources, cost-effective, less control
- Private Cloud: Dedicated resources, more secure, higher cost
- Hybrid Cloud: Combination of public and private
- Community Cloud: Shared by multiple organizations

## Containerization with Docker
- Package application with all dependencies in lightweight container
- Docker Image: Blueprint or template for container
- Docker Container: Running instance of image
- Benefits: Consistency across environments, fast deployment, resource efficiency
- Docker Compose: Define and run multi-container applications
- Container Registry: Central repository for storing images

## Container Orchestration
- Kubernetes (K8s): Automated management of containerized applications
- Manages scaling, updates, networking, and failures
- Pod: Smallest deployable unit containing one or more containers
- Replica Set: Ensures specified number of pods running
- Service: Exposes pods to network
- Deployment: Declarative way to manage pods

## CI/CD Pipeline
- Continuous Integration: Developers merge code frequently with automated tests
- Continuous Deployment: Automatically deploy to production
- Pipeline Stages: Code push, build, test, deploy to staging, approval, deploy to production
- Automation reduces manual errors and accelerates delivery
- Tools: Jenkins (orchestration), GitHub Actions, GitLab CI
- Benefits: Faster releases, fewer bugs, quick feedback

## Infrastructure as Code (IaC)
- Manage infrastructure through code and scripts instead of manual configuration
- Tools: Terraform, Ansible, CloudFormation
- Benefits: Version control, reproducibility, consistency, rapid provisioning
- Enables infrastructure to be treated like software
- Reduces human error and improves reliability

## Monitoring and Logging
- Log Aggregation: Collect logs from multiple sources
- ELK Stack: Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana for log management
- Monitoring Tools: Prometheus, Datadog, New Relic
- Metrics: CPU usage, memory, disk, network, request rate, latency
- Alerts: Automatic notifications for anomalies
- APM (Application Performance Monitoring): Track user experience
- Dashboards: Visualize metrics and trends

## Microservices Architecture
- Break application into small, independent services
- Each service has own database and logic
- Services communicate via APIs
- Benefits: Scalability, flexibility, independent deployment, technology diversity
- Challenges: Network latency, distributed transactions, debugging complexity
- Requires DevOps and container technology for effective management
